sentences.text: Yes, we did have a lot of-- United-- we still have a lot of-- in the United States.
I do have.
For instance, my mother brother-- one of the brothers-- he immigrated to the United States 1900.
He ran away from school.
He couldn't take it.
They used to call him [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, he couldn't take it.
So he ran away.
And he knew he had his great grandmother's sister.
They came over here before civil war yet.
So he ran away to the United States.
These days he didn't need to have an affidavit or things like that-- just got a ticket, and sometime, even without a ticket, they came over here.
So that's why he came over.
He was only 14, 15 years old he came over here.
Emil Mendler.
He lived-- first he lived in New York.
Then he, when he got married, my aunt come from Uniontown.
Then they moved to Latrobe.
And that's where I end up at, in Latrobe--
--after I found him.
I didn't know he lived in Latrobe.
We kept in touch with him, yes, always.
That's why I'm one of the fortunate men.
You know, I have the pictures from my family, because we used to send him pictures and things like this-- correspond always with him.
And that's why I have some of the pictures from previous years.
Of course, my father had also relatives here in New York, you know.
And-- but we didn't keep too much correspondence with him.
I don't know why.
Well, we knew what was happening, what was coming when my father relation came to Poland from Germany-- when they were chased out from Germany.
I won't say chased out.
They had a chance to get out-- came to Poland.
So my father wrote to his uncle.
This is my grandfather brother.
They live in New York.
They still use the name Reibeisen.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I changed my name, but I'll tell you after a while why.
And my father begged him.
They were pretty well off-- then already they were very well off, as a matter of fact-- not him, but his children-- very well off.
My father begged him to save somebody from the farm-- my brother for instance.
You know, my father want to send my brother to the United States.
He was already 16 years old, something like that.
And no response-- very, very few.
That's why I don't have much contact with him, you know?
Yeah.
How did you know that there was-- how did your father know that there was danger coming?
Were there any incidents in Nowy Targ?
Well, we knew because they come from Germany.
So we knew.
We were, yes.

For instance, in 1933, we have a little pogrom in our town, which was nothing unusual, because some towns, they always have pogroms-- not always, but you heard about it-- I'm sure, here in the United States, too.
I remember this was during the summertime, because we were on vacation out of school.
And me and my brother-- I was just a little fellow.
I must have been about 8 years old-- something like that-- 7 years old, maybe.
We were playing in the park.
The park was not too far from my home-- from my father's factory, actually.

In 1933, this was in July, I believe, that little program-- what started, the farmers start to complain to the government they didn't get enough pay for the product they brought to the-- to the market, and so forth on.
But what was the end?
They're always Jews.
Then they start to break the windows in Jewish homes, beat-- beat up the Jewish people, burn the houses, you know, or something.
So my father sent one of our helpers, one of his helpers to the park-- not to come home.
Stay in the park.
And he was our-- I ride with us-- me and my brother.
And he won't let us to come home till everything quiet down.
Was anyone killed?
No, nobody was killed, but it was-- but a lot of damage was done to our Jewish homes.
Well, that's what I said.
They were looted--
--and things like that.
After the police came in from different towns, quiet down.
They chased the farmers back to the-- to the villages.
Fortunately, you know, we were always Jewish.
People were always scapegoats-- which we were.
First thing-- something-- always-- we were always blamed for everything.
It's not so much the farmers, it's the anti-Semite, the Andechs, what we used to call them.
They were the one.
Andechs-- they were people-- they were anti-Semite against the Jewish people.
They used to-- for instance, they used to picket our stores-- Jewish businesses-- don't buy by the Jews.
Jews are Communist.
They are Communist.
They-- they cause all kind of-- they wouldn't allow the Polish people to shop at Jewish businesses.
They were from our town, yes.
Yes.
Yes.
They did.
Well, the head of the-- as a matter-- with the Germans?
From the beginning, they were very well, you know.
But after a while, the Germans didn't need them, you know, as far as that.
As a matter of fact, the head of the-- the anti-Semite organization, he was a guy-- he delivered me.
You know, there wasn't too many doctors in that town. you know?
There was a one-- two Jewish doctors in our town-- three Jewish doctors, actually-- as far as that.
But he was one-- one of the best doctors.
And most of his clientele, they were Jewish people.
He used to shop himself-- he used to come to our house, drink the soda, because we were the only one who had it-- this business.
[NON-ENGLISH], Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH]--
--was his name.
Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH].
Well, I don't know really what happened to him, because I think he escaped also.
I don't know what happened to him.
I didn't see him in town during the war.
Zakopane-- when I was in-- working down in the stone quarry-- they used to pick up so many people and went to stone quarry.
This was our job.
My job, for instance, you know, used to blow out the stones.
It was a granite stone, a very hard stone.
My job was they gave me a hammer, a small hammer, and I had to break the big pieces for smaller pieces.
Every day I had to produce so much.
God forbid if I didn't, you know.
So I used to break the stones with the hammer.
And we used to use the small stones to build the superhighway we built from Zakopane to Krakow.
They were going to go to Germany-- Autobahn.
No.
No.
He used to work in different places--
--on the-- in the roads and the highway in our town, you know.
No.
We used to come once a week, once every two weeks or three weeks sometime.
We used to come on Sunday.
They took us on a truck.
They brought us to town, because it wasn't too far.
So we used to come.
We had a chance to go to see our families-- spend at home day, brought our laundry back, dirty laundry, and spend some day.
And then evening, you had to go back-- OK.
--to the camp.
Actually, we start early in the morning.
We start at 7 o'clock in the morning.
We work till 5, 6 o'clock in the summertime.
Later on, it got darker, so it get a little bit darker.
It was a hard work.
My hand was like a-- like a-- like a stone, especially my fingers were all knuckled up.
You know, you break the stone.
You think it's only you break the small pieces.
It was a hard work.
There were some Polish people and some of the SS troopers.
We live-- we lived down in a camp.
We lived in a home.
It was built like a home.
They took over the buildings-- in we slept.
There were-- there must have been, of us, maybe 40.
40 to 50 people, yeah--
--from our town.
And a lot-- a lot of people got killed down there for no reason.
I'll never forget.
The punishment, yeah.
They used to shoot them.
They used to shoot them.
First they beat them, then shoot them, you know?
If you don't produce-- where they, you know, for instance, my side to produce so much stone every day.
And the food was lousy.
It was-- the ration was very poor.
Bread-- bread and the water, and coffee-- you know what I mean-- and soup-- three times a day.
Zakopane-- I must about two months.
Then I-- oh, then I was assigned.
Then they changed the people.
After a while, they brought different people.
They'd send us-- go home.
We went to different kind of work.

So after I got through with that, I was sent to-- we used to travel by train, not too far from my home town, another 13 miles-- 13 kilometers-- a small, little village town they called Czarny Dunajec-- capital C-Z-A-R-N-Y dash D-U-N-A-J-E-C.
I used to work in a-- we used to-- it was like a-- like a mine.
We used to mine sand.
We used to dig out sand.
We used to sort small stone, different-- different size of the stones, then till we come to sand.
The company name was the [NON-ENGLISH].
It was a German company.
I used to travel every day home, back and forth on a train.
We start early in the morning.
And we work till evening.
We got home late night.
Well, Judenrat, which means-- Judenrat was a Jewish community center.
It's not-- I mean, it was a center, that's true.
Judenrat-- you know what Judenrat is.
The Judenrat usually was--
In the--
--leadership of a ghetto.
They were leadership of the Jewish people.
And one man was in charge.
His name was [NON-ENGLISH], Leon-- [NON-ENGLISH].
He was in charge to the gang labor, to send people to work.
My father joined later on the Judenrat, also.
My father used to work with the people.
For instance, a-- it was a group of us, you know.
And my father had to deliver us to the work.
Then he went back-- keeping order.
Yes.
There was no changes until about 1941--
--till the ghetto.
Dunajec.
Every night I come back to home, yes.
I worked--
I worked down there for about a-- let's see-- for about a year-- for about a year.
Then-- then I was assigned, also in Czarny Dunajec, to lumber yard.
We used to go.
We used to chop the timber.
We used to bring, with the horses, the timber to the lumber yard, unload them, cut them.
We used to build pre-fab homes.
I worked for [NON-ENGLISH].
It's German company-- lumber yard.
I haven't received a penny.
Nothing.
I was only compensated for the time I was in concentration camps, which I got very little.
And I got a couple hundred dollars.
I worked for [NON-ENGLISH] Company in Auschwitz when I was in Auschwitz.
And their company paid me a little bit--
--nothing to speak of, really.
This was a forced labor camp, yes.
I worked-- before I went to Hobug, in between the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] company and Hobug, I worked on the railroad first.
I used to unload the coal.
And we used to lay-- fix the tracks.
Wintertime, we used to travel on the steam-- steam locomotive and clean out the snow from the-- so the train-- the train could pass for miles and miles.
We used to clean out the snow from the railroad tracks.
Then later I was assigned to Hobug.
I worked for Hobug like this till August 30, 1942.
We used to travel back and forth every day.
The living condition was very hard, because we had-- we were rationed, our Jewish people.
We used to get the-- they used to call [NON-ENGLISH].
And the-- and we were rationed.
They used to get so much bread, so much beans, so much sugar, so much butter.
It was very hard, because of course, my family, we weren't so bad.
Because we had a lot of friends-- farmers-- which we used to deal with them.
They used to come to our house.
Our house was open Thursday with always market in that town.
The farmers used to come.
Wintertime, especially, they used to keep themselves warm.
We used to sit and drink the soda water and the hot tea.
We used to sell, too, hot tea.
So we had a lot of friends who used to once in a while used to bring us potatoes and the butter.
So we had a-- it wasn't so bad.
The only thing I remember was a hardship for my parents-- heat up the place.
We had no-- we weren't allowed to purchase wood by-- from the-- from the Gentile people, or coal.
But when I-- as long as I used to work on the railroad station, you know, I used to unload the coal.
I used to always wear two pairs of pants, one inside out.
I used to wear the knickers.
I used to always load up the pants, my pockets, the inside of the knickers, with coal.
In my jacket.
I used to bring it home.
I could hardly walk.
We has to bring some coal home.
That was tough.
It was very hard.
Not much.
No, we didn't get no mail.
We didn't-- we didn't correspond.
Because we all were together, really, except the neighboring town where my father family was.
So we used to hear from them.
People used to travel once in a while.
And that's where we used to get the news.
But--
--well, activities like-- for instance, sometimes on Sunday, we didn't work.
So we used to get together.
This was more already later on in the ghetto-- when we were in the ghetto.
We used to get together, young children, like young kids.
We were already-- we were young kids, but we were grow up already, you know, after--
--all of those years, you know, you become self-sufficient.
You grow up very quick.
The ghetto was performed 1941--
--in our town.
We all lived-- of course, my family didn't move together.
What happened, we used to trade our homes with our gentile friends.
And we were already in process to trade our home.
Then it was a little bit too late already.
This was 1942.
So our family-- there were few-- very few family who lived outside the ghetto yet.
You know.
Oh yes.
Because, I mean, our ghetto was open.
It wasn't fenced in like in different cities, you know-- the ghetto was fenced in with the wires, and the bricks, and mortar, and stuff like that.
But our ghetto was open.
The only difference was all the Jewish people who lived in this part of the section of the town, the Polish people were there to trade their homes.
They had to get out, and the Jewish people were moved in.
But you-- if you owned your property, you had a chance to trade in your homes.
Well, I don't know about that.
They just-- it was a privilege, because we had a little bit more lean than others, you know?
You know, the mayor in our town, and so forth on--
--they help us in a way.
So we don't have to-- we won't have to move.
You know, how can you move?
You know, you don't want to leave your home.
After a while, then those things started.
September-- no, August the 30-- finally-- I remember the day before-- August the 29.
I came home from my job, from Hobug.
This was late at night.
Already it was 10 o'clock, because we used to travel by the train.
We had to take the last train-- what was-- and we didn't travel in a regular train.
We traveled in a caboose, like a regular-- like a freight train for us.
It was-- we weren't allowed to-- we weren't allowed to travel with-- with the other people.
Jewish people had to be separate.
So we got home.
We walk from the station.
From our station to my home was, like, maybe three miles.
We walk home.
And I come home.
The whole family was already home.
My father was.
And besides that, you know, in our neighborhood, the Jewish people used to live in the villages, too.
A couple of days before that, they brought all the Jewish people from the small villages to our-- to our town.
Our town was a county seat.
Let's put it this way.
So-- but all the people-- all the Jewish people from the villages-- I had an uncle in Zakopane-- two uncles I had.
So him and his wife and two children, then the other uncle, he was a doctor, him and his wife-- they came in.
And they all lived in our house.
We had 19 people living in our house-- in our little five-room house.
So I want you to know how we used to slept.
Some sleep in the bed, some slept in the-- on the floor-- on the straw sack.
We used to make a-- like a mattress-- a sack filled up with straw.
We called it straw sack.
And since we're asleep, we slept toe to-- feet to feet-- like that-- 19 people in one house.
Then beside that, we had three dogs also.
[LAUGHS]
You can't imagine it.
But it was one harmony.
It was pretty nice.
What are you going to do?
You know.
They-- everybody come to our house.
Our house, before the war, as a matter of fact, what they used to call [NON-ENGLISH].
It's a Jewish word.
It was an open house.
Any poor people, any dignitary came to our town, stay in our house.
My parents, they never turned down anybody, never.
Before the war, these people used to come to our house.
They used to play cards every single night.
It was brought-- it had been like this for years, for generations, from my grandfather.
Because he had this kind of business years ago, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So they came to our house and stay over there.
And my aunt used to live across the street.
They-- she had to get out from the house.
She had the two children, my Aunt Mala-- and moved to our house-- my aunt, the older-- my mother's older sister with the two girls.
She was one of the professors of the university that was in Dresden.
They were grown up people already.
They were in their 20's-- late 20s.
They all moved to our house.
They had to get out.
So we lived like that.
August the 30-- when I came home the 28th from work, my father came home.
It was 10 o'clock.
Nobody knew about it, but the people for the-- from the Jewish-- [NON-ENGLISH].
How do you say?
Leadership.
Leadership.
They knew what was going to be.
The order came out-- all the Jews must report to the stadium in the morning by 7 o'clock, 8 o'clock in the morning.
My father had to leave early, because he had to go gather the Jewish people.
He was the councilman in the Jewish community, my dad.
So my father told my family, everybody, what we have to do.
I never forget.
My brother was in [NON-ENGLISH] where my-- where my grandfather used to live-- though my mother was, myself, and my sister.
When I say-- when I said goodbye to my dad, I never forget.
My father said to me, remember who you are.
Never forget where you come from.
Never turn down a poor person.
I'll never forgot those word.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Say goodbye.
You know, kiss me.
And it killed everybody.
He left home 5 o'clock in the morning.
The last time I saw him at the stadium-- and we came to the stadium in the morning-- early morning the next day.
My mother took us, me and my sister, and the whole family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had to-- we had to walk to the stadium.
[CRYING] Excuse me.
[CRYING] We had to walk to the stadium.
And all of the-- all the Jewish people who was-- who could walk, walk.
At the ghetto, there were a lot of Jewish people like that, that were crippled, so they left them behind at home.
So walk.
We came to the stadium.
So there-- we were sorted in different groups.
I had seen my father was standing on one side with the Jewish community leaders together.
When I came, they put me on the other side-- people from 14 to 50 years old.
It meant whoever was healthy.
My mother-- they took my mother and my sister on the other side.
There were three different groups-- elderly, and the people.
And some people, they brought them on the wagons and the wheelbarrow.
Because they had to bring them in.
They put them on the other side, another group.
And our city was right across the railroad station.
And we see the cars.
The railroad car was standing empty.
But we didn't know what it was for.
They told us they're going to transport everybody to different camps.
We have to leave the town.
We're going to go to camps.
So me and my friends-- I used to work in the Hobug worker.
And before we-- before that, in the Hobug worker, we built a barrack.
We didn't know.
They had never told us for who, for what.
It was prepared for us.
They put us in the group where we used to work for the Hobug worker.
So we stay on the one side.
And whatever we had, possession-- my father gave me his silver watch.
He said to me, maybe someday you're going to need it.
So meantime, everything we had, we had to give them back to the Germans.
But what I did-- the chain from the watch, I hid it.
I stuck in my-- underneath my jacket.
Much I had to give him back.
So I gave him this thing.
And they load us up on the truck.
And then my mother and my sister waved goodbye to me.
And my father still was standing down there.
And they took us away back to Czarny Dunajec, to the camp where I worked before.
This was my first concentration camps.
My father was, like I said, he was standing in a different-- a different group.
They took all the Jewish community, the leadership, to the Jewish cemetery in our town, on a truck.
And all the remaining Jewish people in town, the crippled, older one that couldn't walk-- 500 in all-- they took them to the Jewish cemetery and just stripped them naked.
The grave was waiting for them.
They had to chase them.
The chased them all around the cemetery.
They used to beat them, punish them.
Finally, they execute them all.
And that's where my father--
